All kidding aside, though, all of the toxicity of "intersectionality" will emerge here in Texas, but it will expand into every Democrat jurisdiction in upcoming cycles.

WSJ: Rowling, who had contributed more than $750,000 to groups supporting Cornyn, is one of the high-dollar Republican donors who could be key to Paxton’s efforts in a competitive race. But Rowling, a onetime supporter of Paxton, had been turned off by his alleged extramarital affairs and was emotional over his drubbing of Cornyn, he said. He initially declined to help.

Then Rowling got a call from Sen. Ted Cruz, who asked him to meet with Paxton. Rowling said he had begun to consider whether Democratic opponent James Talarico was a concerning enough threat for him to open his wallet again.

He ended up taking a meeting with Paxton and Cruz to hear them out.

Rowling’s hesitation highlights the bind confronting a bloc of deep-pocketed Texans weighing how involved to get in the race. Many can’t stomach Paxton—a populist firebrand dogged by legal and ethical scandals and known for targeting corporate interests. But with control of the Senate in the balance and a well-funded Democrat on the other side, some are deciding they can’t afford to sit this one out. Paxton’s success hinges, in part, on winning them back.

Ed: It's pretty typical to have donors and voters expressing anger and disappointment after a contentious primary. However, Paxton has several weeks to work on assuaging the hard feelings from his challenge to Cornyn. The need to keep the Senate in GOP control will likely move donors back into action. However, the Texas Senate race will clearly take some work from Republicans to secure, regardless of whether Talarico triggers a split among Democrats.

NY Post: Hizzoner, who is deeply skeptical of prisons, said that his team was exploring the legality of detaining Netanyahu should the Israeli prime minister come to the Big Apple for the United Nations General Assembly (UNGA) or another event.

“Well, it’s such a tough guy to say that kind of thing. He has no way to do that,” Fetterman (D-Pa.) chided on Fox News’ “Sunday Morning Futures.”

The mayor cited the International Criminal Court’s (ICC) 2024 arrest warrant for Netanyahu over the war in Gaza.

“Obviously, America’s not even part of that corrupt court, and so he’s just a clown to even say that. So he won’t try that,” Fetterman went on. “And now, just sit down and focus on the problems that they have in New York. That’s really not your purview.”

Noah Rothman at NRO: New York magazine’s David Freedlander assembled a variety of juicy quotes from Democratic insiders for his piece on Kamala Harris’s insatiable hunger for power. Most of those insiders seem only to want Harris to go away.

Some took the diplomatic track. They’d profess their undying affection for the former vice president but also wonder if she can raise the requisite funds or stay focused on the future given her inextricable association with Joe Biden’s administration. Others were less tactful. “I hope she runs,” said one Democratic strategist. “Because then she will lose and we will never have to hear from her again.”

As Freedlander observed, Democratic political professionals are inclined to respond either with that fury or with a “heavy sigh followed by a long pause” when the subject of Harris’s ambitions arises. Democrats haven’t recovered from the trauma they experienced in 2024. And as long as Harris’s advisers continue to torment them with the delusions that they’re deploying to draw Biden’s veep back into the limelight, they won’t.

To the claim that nominating Harris would only force Americans to relitigate the Biden years, for example, her brain trust offers this compelling rejoinder: Nuh-uh.

Ed: Count me in the same camp as the Democrat strategist. Harris is a uniquely incompetent campaigner who will never win a single primary, plus she carries the stink of the Biden Inc fraud perpetrated on America from 2020-2024. She's attempting to gain favor with the DSA, whose influence may have already peaked in this cycle as their radical nature gets exposed. Newsmax: In the wake of multiple fatal shootings involving U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers, the agency will require its officers to record vehicle stops with ‌at least one body camera, Trump administration border czar Tom Homan said Sunday.

“They exonerate more law enforcement than they convict, and I want officers to wear body cameras because I want the American people to see what the officers saw when ⁠they took that action,” Homan said on the "Fox & Friends Weekend” program. ...

In a separate interview on CBS' "Face the Nation" show, Homan said money for the cameras was held up during the partial government shutdown earlier this year.

"The cameras ‌have been purchased right now," Homan said. "They're training the trainer in deploying the cameras nationwide."

DHS got significant capital funds last summer in the One Big Beautiful Bill, around $75 billion, and body cams are standard issue for most other law-enforcement agencies. This is a step that should have preceded operations in Minnesota, and the fact that six months have gone by without correcting this deficiency should have Republicans who support these operations seeing red.

Daily Wire: Controversial influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate on Saturday were arrested by federal officials in Miami pursuant to rape charges and an outstanding arrest warrant in the United Kingdom.

The Crown Prosecution Service last year charged Andrew Tate with 10 counts of crimes, including rape and human trafficking related to three women. His younger brother, Tristan, was charged with 11 counts of similar crimes tied to one woman. On Saturday, the Crown Prosecution Service announced additional counts for each brother, including 19 charges related to illegal child images and pornography for Andrew Tate.

“We can confirm the arrest of the Tate brothers today in Miami,” the U.S. Marshals Service said in a statement. “The warrant was sealed so we are unable to confirm the charges.” The office of Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier — who last year opened an investigation into the Tate brothers — confirmed to The Daily Wire that the pair were taken into custody on an arrest warrant from the United Kingdom.

John Podhoretz at the Free Beacon: Christopher Nolan does something extraordinarily nervy in his overwhelming new film, The Odyssey. He has made as faithful a version of Homer’s poem as could possibly be made in the course of a three-hour running time—all while turning its worldview inside out.

The thing is, detail by detail, The Odyssey is not just faithful, it’s absolutely inspired. It’s possible that this is the single-greatest cinematic adaptation of a great work of literature in the 113-year history of the motion picture as a popular art form.

Nolan hits every story beat, makes staggering visual poetry out of Homer’s oracular poetry, and even finds a way to evoke the twisty-turny narrative through which Homer reveals the story of Odysseus’ 10-year journey home from the Trojan War and the crisis his absence has created in his home kingdom of Ithaca.

But Nolan’s great reveal, at the end of the film, is nowhere to be found in Homer. It offers a very modern psychological explanation for Odysseus’ lostness. 

Ed: Be sure to read it all to get John's conclusory point. Given the way that Hollywood normally mangles premium source material – Peter Jackson's Lord of the Rings is an exception rather than the norm – this claim alone is enough to get people into the cinema. Not having yet seen The Odyssey, I am not prepared to make the argument that LotR is better, but I'd say that even with the anti-Homeric approach John describes, it's a worthy competitor.

news.title: Why Democrats Can't Do Better Than Kamala Harris in 2028
news.text:
There are only two lanes to the Democratic presidential nomination two years from now, and one of them is already blocked — by Kamala Harris.

Get ready for the Kamala comeback, followed, in all likelihood, by a replay of her crushing defeat by the Republican nominee.

Believe it or not, the ex-VP occupies what counts as the "center lane" within the party, and for most of the last three decades that's the one that's led to the nomination.

Republicans used to be the party of orderly succession, even if the candidate whose turn had come looked like a certain loser: think Bob Dole in '96.

But recently, it's been Democrats who've tended to pick a party elder as their nominee:

Al Gore, as a sitting VP, might not have been remarkable in 2000, but in '04 it was John Kerry, in '16 Hillary Clinton, and in 2020 Joe Biden — with only Barack Obama butting ahead of the line in 2008.

Democrats like a known quantity, a reliable liberal who isn't too overtly radical; the radicalism can wait until after Election Day.

So they're leaning to running Harris again, even if donors aren't happy about it: They blew nearly $2 billion on her campaign and aligned PACs in 2024, only for Harris to lose every battleground state to Trump.

It won't be donors who decide the party's 2028 nominee, though, and ordinary Democrats actually find Harris reassuring.

She's a familiar face, after all, commanding practically universal name recognition.

Harris checks the identity-politics boxes that may be as important as winning to Democratic primary voters: Their ticket must have a woman, and it can't be all white.

And after being vetted as a VP and undergoing the trial-by-fire of the '24 campaign, Harris isn't likely to surprise voters with any extremist tattoos or sexual-assault allegations — things that can't be taken for granted among Democrats nowadays.

Then there's the revenge motive: What better way to repudiate the Trump era and reverse the '24 humiliation than by making the woman he defeated president?

Democratic primary voters are still smarting from Hillary Clinton's drubbing in 2016: by 2028, the election of America's first female president will have been postponed 12 long years, and feminists in the party aren't willing to wait any longer.

These are the reasons Harris tops almost all polls of 2028 Democratic hopefuls, with an 11.6-point lead in the RealClearPolitics polling aggregate as of mid-July.

"She comes in with 99 percent name ID and everyone else is going to have to raise money and buy it," a former Harris aide tells New York's David Freedlander.

The donors might prefer just about anyone else, yet they'd have to pour an enormous amount of cash into another candidate just to make him or her recognizable.

This has led one longtime Democratic strategist with donors' ears to welcome the inevitable: "I hope she runs," Alex Hoffman told New York, "Because then she will lose and we will never have to hear from her again. She will never win an election at a national scale. Never."

But if Harris is hopeless, why does she seem inevitable?

She has seniority no one else can match, but what about the other lane that leads to the nomination?

That's the left lane — the far left lane.

The one Bernie Sanders occupied in 2020 and 2016, and that a candidate like Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez might fill in 2028.

Young Dems keep moving leftward, with the Democratic Socialists of America making deeper inroads into the party.

Harris is not nearly left-wing enough for them — but then, anyone who is would be upsetting to the old guard.

It's not that the old-timers aren't also well to the left on economics, but they don't want to be labeled "socialists"— while the insurgent left is proud of the brand.

The party's mainstream wants to have it both ways on the Israel-Palestine conflict — while the young left, increasingly, is explicit about its anti-Zionism.

There are still enough baby boomers and suburbanites in the Democratic Party to make the left lane an unlikely road to the nomination. For now.

Harris can expect a challenge from the left, but the party is still probably one more presidential cycle away from nominating someone who openly represents where Democrats are headed.

In fact, a Harris defeat in 2028 would be the perfect prelude to the socialist and anti-Zionist left's takeover, discrediting the old guard for forfeiting the election to Donald Trump's successor.

Insider Democrats know Kamala Harris is a terrible candidate, but the party they built can't tell her no — even if this is the last nomination they get to make before DSA takes over.

news.title: Obama warns Vance is pushing 'blood and soil' version of 'We the People' mantra
news.text: Former President Barack Obama suggested Thursday that Vice President JD Vance was pushing a "blood and soil" vision for the country despite being married to the "daughter of an immigrant." The vice president came up as Obama was discussing the Reconstruction era with author Malcolm Gladwell, claiming that the issues from that era are still being discussed today. "It’s impossible to spend a lot of time thinking about Reconstruction without reflecting on the present day, right?" Obama said. "Because these issues are — well, they’re still there. If somebody is asking why it is relevant to talk about Reconstruction, I have to describe for them the degree to which we’re having the same arguments. The issues of: How are we going to help the freed people who’ve been enslaved? What are we going to give them to allow them to have a go at life? What does real freedom mean?" 'GROSS' SLATE PIECE COMES UNDER FIRE FOR SUGGESTING USHA VANCE PREGNANCY IS POLITICAL STRATEGY He argued that more directly, they continue to debate over voting rights after the Supreme Court ruling on race-based congressional gerrymandering as well as Vance's political party pushing for a "certain kind of people." "We have a politics now that at least one of our major parties has been captured by politics that is not that subtle about suggesting that 'We the people' means a certain kind of people," Obama said. "When you have the vice president, current vice president, making a speech that is basically a blood and soil version of 'We the people,' that it matters who your parents were and how long they've been here despite him being married to a daughter of an immigrant himself." He continued, "That echoes, then, ideas about who can be a citizen, who belongs, who gets to make decisions. So all these issues are still being debated, sometimes not as bluntly and explicitly as they have in the past, but they're still there." OBAMA CLAIMS HE OCCUPIES 'A SUITE' IN TRUMP'S MIND, QUESTIONS THE PRESIDENT'S FOCUS ON AMERICANS "Blood and soil" refers to a phrase popularized by Nazi Germany and associated with White nationalism. Gladwell went on to accuse Vance of "hypocrisy" for attempting to make a "nativist argument" while being married to Usha Vance, the daughter of Indian immigrants. Obama agreed that Vance was a hypocrite but conceded that it was progress compared to a century prior. "That is progress. Listen, hypocrisy is progress. It is, because it means that you feel guilty enough to either lie to yourself or others. And that is better than not even thinking about the idea that maybe you’re doing something wrong," Obama said. Fox News Digital reached out to Vance's office for comment. Vance's most recent speech was earlier this month honoring America's 250th birthday on July 4 in New York. During his remarks, he urged Americans to reject the "two-dimensional view" of the country based on its failings. 'OBAMA BRO' PODCASTER RAILS AGAINST JD VANCE'S CHANCES IN 2028, CLAIMS 'NO ONE LIKES HIM!' "So what I'd ask you to do, my fellow Americans, on our 250th birthday, is to reject the two-dimensional view of your fellow citizens and reject the two-dimensional view of your country. Reject that America is a place for zero-sum thinking because it is not. Our history is one of people carving a great civilization out of the wilderness. Reject the view of your nation that sees only its sins, but not its grace and its greatness. Everything that we have done, everything that we've done as a country, we have all done together, not as citizens divided against each other, but as a common people working towards a common future," Vance said. Obama has largely avoided discussing Vance directly in public comments. He previously took a subtle swipe against Vance during the 2024 election after then-Vice President Kamala Harris chose Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz as a running mate. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP "When a presidential candidate chooses a running mate, it says a lot about who they are and what kind of president they'll be. Do they pick someone inexperienced and polarizing who will deepen our divisions?" Obama asked. "Or do they pick someone with the judgment to make tough decisions, and the character to believe that every voice counts and everyone deserves an equal chance?"
news.summary: Former President Barack Obama accused Vice President JD Vance of pushing a "blood and soil" vision of America despite being married to the daughter of an immigrant.
news.author: Lindsay Kornick

news.title: Remember When Kamala Harris Wanted Paper Ballots for Our Elections?
news.text:
With Democrats in high dudgeon over the SAVE America Act and voter integrity, we'd do well to remember there was a time when Kamala Harris ran on a platform of getting rid of voting machines and using just paper ballots.

In 2019, Kamala Harris ran on getting rid of US voting machines in favor of fully paper ballotsI wonder what changed in 2020 pic.twitter.com/rbQVIewTHe— End Wokeness (@EndWokeness) July 19, 2026

"The best and most secure way to conduct elections? Paper ballots," she said. "Cause the way that I say it, kind of half joking, Russia can't hack a piece of paper."

In another appearance, Kamala restated her opinion in multiple appearances on the campaign trail.

"We have a bill that basically would require states to upgrade their election infrastructure because guess what? Going like back to the future, the best and smartest way to conduct voting? Paper ballots," Kamala said.

"Paper ballots. I joke and I say paper ballots because Russia can't hack a piece of paper!"

Kamala said it again, in a third clip. "The most secure way to vote? Paper. You're right. And, you know ... it would be great if the Republican leader would put these bills on the floor for a vote."

Even Whoopi Goldberg and the audience of 'The View' agreed with her.

Kamala was so excited about paper ballots that she went on The View and cackled about it.Whoopi agreed. Audience applauded. https://t.co/bE1ZDBbU2L pic.twitter.com/YHaVh7rZ6O— Western Lensman (@WesternLensman) July 19, 2026

"And you said something, I have been saying forever and ever and I'm going to say it in 45 seconds," Goldberg said. "You've been saying that it's time for us to go back to paper ballots. Why?"

"Because Russia can't hack a piece of paper," Kamala replied.

"There you go," Goldberg said.

Kamala Harris clearly believes Russia stole the 2016 election. That's funny, because we've been told repeatedly that questioning the outcomes of the election is an existential threat to our democracy, and grounds for going to jail, being stripped of your law license, and sued in civil court.

What's (D)ifferent between 2020 and 2026?

news.title: Megyn Kelly Is Giddy About Kamala Harris Possibly Running for President Again: ‘You Go, Girl’
news.text:
Megyn Kelly is giddy at the possibility of Kamala Harris running for president again.

On Monday’s episode of “The Megyn Kelly Show,” the host could hardly contain her joy while discussing reports that Harris may throw her hat into the ring to run for president again after losing to Donald Trump in 2024. It was clearly an idea she was fond of because she felt like it was a guaranteed win for Republicans.

“There are already major developments in the race to be the Democratic nominee for president in 2028. We’re rooting, of course, for Kamala to give it another go, and it does sound like that may be happening,” Kelly said. “Because look at this headline from New York Magazine this morning: ‘Like It or Not, Kamala Harris Is the Front-runner.’ You go, girl. You got this. Don’t believe the naysayers. Look at New York Magazine. They are acknowledging you’re the frontrunner. That’s right. Despite cackling through a billion dollars in 107 days, only to fail by losing every single swing state, the Democrats are seriously considering giving Kamala Harris another. I love them so much. Thank you, Democrats.”

She added: “Let her run! The more America saw Kamala Harris, of course, the less they liked her as a presidential candidate, but who cares? Because she’s a woman and she’s black, and if you are a Democrat, that’s all that matters. But Harris’s advisers somehow came to the opposite conclusion that she was building momentum with voters, determining that the problem for her in losing was simply that she didn’t have enough time to show America who she really was. The problem is not that we got to know her and didn’t like her, is that we didn’t get to know her.”

Kelly made her position on Harris clear throughout the 108 days she ran in opposition of Trump in the election following Joe Biden’s dropout. Back in 2024, the SiriusXM host even went so far as to say that electing Harris as president of the United States would only set women back.

“This person is weak and she is stupid,” Kelly said. “She is not a smart person and far from a deep thinker. She will get eaten alive by Putin and Xi. No one will fear us. She cannot make decisions. The 3 a.m. phone call will leave her paralyzed with fear and indecision. She will fail in her negotiations with our adversaries because she cannot think on her feet. The presidency is not a job you can do for four years with training wheels.”

She finished: “They say Kamala Harris will break barriers as the first woman president, but her election will set women back decades because she is not a smart person.”

Kelly is still clearly rooted deeply in the conservative side of politics but has suffered her own falling out with President Trump in the last few months surrounding the war with Iran. The host is clearly looking to the future and the hopes of the next president also being a Republican.
news.summary:
"The more America saw Kamala Harris, of course, the less they liked her as a presidential candidate, but who cares," the host adds

news.title: How Bernie Sanders Finally Won
news.text: Vermont Senator Bernie Sanders lost the Democratic presidential primary twice, in 2016 and again in 2020. Both times, the party's establishment closed ranks around a more moderate nominee, and Sanders went home to Burlington. A decade later, the argument he built his campaigns on, that the Democratic Party had grown too cozy with corporate donors and too timid on health care, is no longer a fringe position inside the party. It is close to a consensus one. Polling now shows Democratic voters embracing the label "socialist" at levels unseen in modern American politics, and Medicare for All and higher taxes on the wealthy testing as some of the most popular ideas the party has to offer. Sanders, meanwhile, remains the most popular sitting senator in the country, in a state with a Republican governor. Bernie Sanders' Signature Issues Are Now Mainstream in the Polls Some Democratic strategists were writing obituaries for the Sanders wing of the party as recently as the 2020 primary, when Sanders lost the nomination to Joe Biden after a moderate consolidation. Six years later, that wing is arguably running the party's energy, even if it has not yet run the table. The clearest evidence of the shift is in how Democratic voters describe themselves. An I&I/TIPP poll conducted in late May found 46 percent of Democrats view socialism favorably, compared with 32 percent unfavorably. An Economist/YouGov survey in late June found 58 percent of Democrats view socialism favorably overall, and 62 percent said they would vote for a candidate who identified as a Democratic Socialist. Gallup's most recent measure found 66 percent of Democrats view socialism positively, compared with 42 percent who view capitalism positively, a reversal from a decade ago. Health care shows a similar pattern. A Data for Progress survey found 65 percent of likely voters, including 78 percent of Democrats and 71 percent of independents, support creating a Medicare for All-style national health insurance program. A separate Data for Progress poll of persuadable swing voters this month found raising taxes on the wealthy and creating a Medicare for All system were the two policies most likely to move an undecided voter toward a Democratic candidate this fall. Taxing the rich, another Sanders staple, now polls closer to consensus than controversy. A Navigator Research survey found 79 percent of Americans, including 63 percent of Republicans, support raising taxes on the wealthy. A separate analysis from the Excessive Wealth Disorder Institute found two-thirds of Americans back a billionaire income tax, including a majority of Republicans. Socialist and Progressive Candidates Are Multiplying Across the Country The clearest sign of the Sanders coalition's reach is not in the polling. It is on the ballot. New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani, a democratic socialist, has become the most visible face of the movement's electoral wing. His former campaign strategist, Morris Katz, went on to help launch the Senate campaign in Maine for Graham Platner, an oyster farmer and veteran who ran as an economic populist against Republican Senator Susan Collins before dropping out this month amid allegations of sexual assault, which he has not fully addressed publicly. In Michigan, Abdul El-Sayed is running for the Democratic Senate nomination against Representative Haley Stevens, a race that has become another proxy fight over the party's direction. In New York, Democratic Socialists of America-endorsed candidates knocked off established incumbents in multiple House primaries this cycle, including Darializa Avila Chevalier's defeat of longtime Representative Adriano Espaillat. In Minnesota, the open Senate primary between Lieutenant Governor Peggy Flanagan and Representative Angie Craig has become a proxy contest between Sanders-style progressivism and the party's centrist wing, with Senate Democratic leadership reportedly favoring Craig. The Democratic Socialists of America says it has been involved in more than 130 races this cycle, with dozens of candidates still on primary ballots heading into the fall. Joseph Geevarghese, executive director of the progressive group Our Revolution, said the appeal is no longer ideological in nature. It is a reaction to what voters see as a government that answers to corporations rather than to them, he said. "Red Scare tactics only work on people who haven't lived through what unchecked corporate power actually does," Geevarghese told Newsweek. "This generation has. They're not falling in love with an ideology. They're done with a government that answers to corporations instead of to them." Can Bernie Sanders' Message Win Democrats the Midterms? The Democratic brand itself is in historically poor shape heading into that argument. A CNN poll this year found the party's favorability at 28 percent, its worst mark in that survey's history dating to 1992. Jim Kessler, executive vice president for policy at the center-left think tank Third Way, said midterms are typically decided by turnout rather than persuasion. "When extremists win the nomination it juices turnout on the other end of the ideological spectrum as voters feel their values threatened," he told Newsweek, pointing to Mamdani's mayoral win, in which he drew more votes than any winning New York City mayoral candidate in history, but also more opposing votes than any elected mayor in the city's history. President Donald Trump's approval rating remains low, and voters are unsatisfied with the economy, the Iran war, inflation and tariffs, Kessler told Newsweek, which should favor Democrats if the election becomes a referendum on the party in power. Republicans' only viable bet, he said, is turning the race into "a choice between Republicans and an extremist Democratic Party," pointing to Platner's collapsed Maine campaign as the kind of self-inflicted wound that could hand them that argument. "For midterm voters this could be an election where they are forced to pick their poison," Kessler said. Sanders remains the data point neither side can fully explain away. He holds the highest approval rating of any senator in the country, in a small, largely rural state that also elects a Republican governor. Whether that is a template Democrats can replicate nationally, or a singular result tied to one senator's decades-long relationship with Vermont voters, is the question both wings of the party are still arguing about, months before midterm voters render their own verdict. Can the Sanders Coalition Win Competitive Elections? Polls suggest Sanders' ideas have moved closer to the Democratic mainstream. But some party strategists argue that popularity within the Democratic base does not necessarily translate into victories in the battleground districts that decide control of Congress. Kessler sees the same polling very differently. The bloc of Democratic Socialists of America members heading to Congress next session will remain a small fraction of the overall Democratic caucus, Kessler told Newsweek. But their geography matters more than their headcount, he said. "June wins in New York City came in districts that on average supported Kamala Harris by 36 points," Kessler said, contrasting that with the House seats Democrats need to flip to regain the majority, which he said average Trump plus 8 points. "They are winning races in only the most fertile territory and often against an incumbent Democrat who has been in office too long and has been caught napping." Kessler acknowledged that the word "socialist" no longer scares off as many voters as it once did, largely because the Cold War is ancient history for much of the electorate. But he argued the movement's underlying positions still carry significant risk in competitive districts. "Democratic Socialists hold some very unpopular positions that, in anything resembling a competitive district, would mean certain defeat," Kessler told Newsweek, citing calls to defund the police, open borders, abolish federal prisons and nationalize large sectors of the economy. As one example, Kessler pointed to Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed, whom he views as a liability in a swing state. He also dismissed the chances of Democratic Socialists winning the party's 2028 presidential nomination as "somewhere between slim and none," adding that if they did, "Republicans will romp to victory." Contact Newsweek editors on this story: Jason Lemon and Sam Wilson.
news.summary: Bernie Sanders lost two presidential bids, but his ideas are now popular with Democrats. Can they win elections?
news.author: Jesus Mesa

news.title: Troy Jackson on track to replace Platner as Democratic Senate nominee in Maine
news.text:
Former Maine Senate President Troy Jackson appears on track to clinch the Democratic nomination for Senate after his top competitors made weekend exits from the race.

Jackson had the support of a vast majority of delegates who were elected across the state’s counties over the weekend to serve at a convention Saturday to pick a replacement for Graham Platner, a progressive who won the nomination in a June primary but ended his campaign earlier this month in the face of a sexual assault allegation.

“We’re well on our way to getting the government that we f---ing deserve,” Jackson said in a video of him speaking to supporters posted to social media Saturday.

A logger from Allagash in northern Maine, Jackson ran for governor this year, placing third in the ranked-choice Democratic primary. He had campaigned alongside Platner and was backed by the progressive group Our Revolution and California Rep. Ro Khanna, as well as several labor groups. 

Like Platner, Jackson has centered his campaign on working-class issues. He has called for “Medicare for All” and dismantling the Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency and has pledged not to vote for sending U.S. taxpayer dollars to aid Israel's military. 

While the elected delegates aren’t bound to vote for a specific candidate, other Democratic hopefuls were quick to drop out of the contest and rally around Jackson in the race to take on longtime Republican Sen. Susan Collins.

“I am stepping aside, and I am doing it for exactly the same reason that led me to get in: we must defeat Susan Collins, and the Democratic Party must be unified to do it,” Nirav Shah, a former director of the Maine Center for Disease Control and Prevention who also ran for governor, said in a statement Sunday. 

Shah’s announcement followed one earlier in the day from another former gubernatorial candidate, Shenna Bellows, who said she wanted to focus on her work as Maine secretary of state. Jordan Wood, a former Capitol Hill aide, and Dan Kleban, a co-founder of Maine Beer Company, also ended their campaigns Sunday and said they would support Jackson.

“This is a man who cares deeply about showing up for his community and for all of us,” Wood, who lost the Democratic primary for the battleground 2nd District last month, said in a video shared on social media. “I know that Troy is somebody who has the courage, not just to beat Susan Collins, but to serve our state in the U.S. Senate.”

Seven other candidates remained in the race on Monday, according to the Maine Democratic Party’s website, including David Costello, who placed third in the June Senate primary. 

Delegates will officially pick the party’s next nominee Saturday at a convention in Bangor. But Jackson’s apparent lead in delegate support suggests it may not be as drawn-out of an affair as was expected. His campaign said 481 of the 500 delegates who were elected at county conventions this weekend were supporting the former state legislator. 

Assuming he secures the nomination, Jackson will move on to a general election matchup against Collins, the only Senate Republican representing a state won by Kamala Harris in 2024. He’ll have to unite Democrats across the state who may be shaken from Platner’s abrupt rise and fall in the face of a rape allegation from a former girlfriend, which he has denied. 

Inside Elections with Nathan L. Gonzales rates the Senate race in Maine a Toss-up.
news.author: Mary Ellen McIntire

news.title: Catholic Bishops Ask Trump to Change IVF Insurance Rule
news.text: Leaders of a key part of President Donald Trump's political coalition are urging his administration to rethink a major proposal designed to expand access to fertility treatments, opening a new fault line between the White House's support for in vitro fertilization (IVF) and the leaders of one of the Republican Party's most important swing voting groups. But the Trump administration's stance may be more in line with views of mainstream Catholics than those held by the bishops. The U.S. Conference of Catholic Bishops (USCCB) submitted formal comments this week opposing portions of a proposed federal rule that would make it easier for employers to offer fertility benefits, including IVF coverage, as a separate category of health benefits. In a July 13 letter to federal regulators, the bishops argued that while infertility is a serious medical condition deserving treatment, IVF should not be included in the policy because it conflicts with Catholic teachings about human life and reproduction. The comments place the Catholic hierarchy at odds with one of Trump's most high-profile family-policy initiatives. Trump has repeatedly backed expanding access to IVF, arguing that fertility treatments can help address family-building challenges at a time when the United States is experiencing historically low birth rates. Catholics played a significant role in Trump's 2024 victory, and he won a majority of Catholic voters nationwide. Yet the Catholic Church remains firmly opposed to IVF, setting up a potential clash between a Republican administration seeking to promote fertility treatments and influential religious leaders within its own voter base. White House spokesperson Allison Schuster told Newsweek that the Trump administration is making it a "priority to address the infertility crisis at its core." She pointed to the MAHA Report, which outlined strategies to combat infertility through diet, reducing chemical exposure and through other means before going to IVF. “President Trump promised to help American families struggling to have children, and his Administration has taken an aggressive approach to support family formation," Schuster said. "Never before has a President made such efforts to investigate the primary causes behind the infertility crisis.” Why Are Catholic Bishops Opposed to IVF? The Catholic Church's opposition to IVF is rooted in long-standing teachings about human life, marriage and reproduction. In their submission, the bishops argued that IVF separates procreation from the marital act and results in the destruction or freezing of human embryos. The letter contends that many embryos created during IVF cycles never result in live births and that the process frequently involves selecting which embryos are implanted and which are discarded or frozen indefinitely. The bishops also argued that IVF does not actually treat infertility itself. Instead, they described the procedure as bypassing underlying reproductive conditions rather than curing them. Rather than IVF, the bishops urged the administration to promote what they call "restorative reproductive medicine," an approach focused on diagnosing and treating conditions that contribute to infertility, including hormonal disorders, endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome and other medical issues. The filing praises portions of the proposed rule that would allow coverage for diagnostic services and treatments aimed at addressing root causes of infertility. The bishops encouraged regulators to strengthen those provisions while excluding IVF and other assisted reproductive technologies. "One IVF clinic suggests that someone in their mid-thirties will need about 11 or 12 eggs, 80 percent of which are mature for fertilization, to achieve one live birth. In consideration of these numbers, promoting IVF thus stands in glaring contrast to this Administration’s other pro-life statements and actions," the bishops wrote in their letter. The bishops' position reflects official Catholic doctrine, which teaches that children should be conceived through the marital relationship rather than through laboratory procedures. While church leaders express compassion for couples dealing with infertility, they maintain that not every technological solution is morally acceptable. Their opposition also extends beyond IVF itself. Catholic leaders have frequently raised concerns about embryo freezing, genetic screening of embryos and so-called "selective reduction" procedures when multiple embryos implant successfully. How Important Are Catholics to Republicans? The disagreement underscores the political significance of Catholic voters for Republicans. Catholics have historically been one of the nation's most important swing voting blocs, often closely mirroring the broader electorate. But recent elections have shown a noticeable movement toward Republicans. According to a Pew Research Center voter analysis released in 2025, Trump won approximately 55 percent of Catholic voters in the 2024 election, compared with 43 percent for then-Vice President Kamala Harris. That represented a significant shift from 2020, when the Catholic vote was nearly evenly divided between then-presidential candidate Joe Biden and Trump. The political importance of Catholic voters extends beyond their numbers. Catholics make up roughly one-fifth of the U.S. electorate and have substantial influence in key battleground states, including Pennsylvania, Wisconsin and Michigan. Many Catholic voters are drawn to Republican positions on abortion, religious liberty and other social issues. At the same time, Catholics are not politically monolithic. Surveys have found substantial differences between practicing Catholics who attend Mass regularly and Catholics who identify culturally with the faith but are less active in religious life. The IVF debate illustrates the complexity of that coalition. Polling has generally found broad public support for IVF, including among many Republican voters. Some surveys have also shown support for IVF among rank-and-file Catholics, even though church leaders remain opposed. Sixty-five percent of Catholics said IVF was a good thing in a Pew Research poll from 2024, and a study from 2025 found 83 percent of Catholics think the church should let couples use IVF. That support is higher among people who don't attend Mass frequently, but 71 percent of Catholics who attend church weekly support people undergoing IVF. That creates a unique challenge for Trump. Expanding IVF access may be popular among the broader electorate and consistent with his efforts to address concerns about falling birth rates. Yet it places him at odds with Catholic bishops who share the administration's views on many other life and family issues, and that disagreement can trickle down through the church. What Pope Leo Has Said About IVF Pope Leo XIV has not made IVF a major focus of his papacy, but there is little indication that he intends to depart from traditional Catholic teachings on reproductive technologies. Since becoming pope in 2025, Leo has repeatedly emphasized the importance of family life, fertility and openness to children. He has spoken about concerns surrounding declining birth rates and praised the role of marriage and parenthood in society. While he has not issued a major standalone statement focused specifically on IVF, the Vatican's official position remains unchanged. Catholic teaching continues to hold that IVF is morally impermissible because it separates conception from the marital act and often results in the destruction or indefinite freezing of embryos. Leo has also frequently cited the church's broader teachings on the dignity of human life and the importance of family relationships. As a result, there is little expectation among Vatican observers that the current pope would support efforts to expand IVF access in ways that conflict with longstanding church doctrine. That places the bishops' objections squarely within mainstream Catholic teaching rather than representing a uniquely American disagreement with the Trump administration. What Trump's Proposed Rule Would Change About IVF The proposed regulation does not create a federal IVF mandate. Instead, the rule would establish a new category of "excepted fertility benefits" that employers could choose to offer separately from traditional health insurance coverage. Supporters say the change would make it easier for businesses to provide fertility-related benefits while potentially lowering administrative burdens associated with existing health-plan requirements. The proposal covers a broad range of fertility services, including diagnostic testing, treatments addressing medical causes of infertility and assisted reproductive technologies like IVF. Administration officials have framed the effort as part of a broader push to support family formation and improve access to fertility care. The bishops acknowledged that portions of the proposal could help families struggling with infertility. Their comments specifically praised language recognizing the importance of treating underlying reproductive health conditions and encouraging efforts to address the causes of infertility. However, they argued that regulators should draw a distinction between fertility treatments that restore reproductive function and IVF procedures that bypass biological barriers to conception. Whether federal agencies will modify the proposal remains unclear. For now, the filing highlights a delicate political balancing act. Trump's administration continues to position itself as both pro-family and pro-IVF, while influential Catholic leaders are urging federal officials to steer fertility policy in a different direction. But based on polling among mainstream Catholics, it's possible that the Trump administration's position on IVF could win over more Catholics than it pushes away. Contact Newsweek editors on this story: Edward T. Cummins.
news.summary: Trump's plan to combat the declining birth rate is putting him at odds with the leaders of a key swing voting bloc.
news.author: Jenni Fink

news.title: 'More bad news for Trump' as his popularity tanks among essential voting bloc
news.text:
Mothers have had it with President Donald Trump. This is according to the latest polling, which found that parents in general have made a dramatic move away from Trump, with moms accounting for the biggest shift. 

The polling shows that since July 2025, support for Trump has plunged by almost 30 points among moms, resulting in a net approval of -20. It also found that the majority of fathers disapprove of him. Overall, the new poll found that 61 percent of voters are negative on Trump, resulting in a total net approval of -23. 

According to the Daily Beast, “It’s more bad news for Trump heading into the midterms, as cost of living concerns drive voters, and their opinions of him and his administration. Mothers are often in charge of the family budget, managing household bills and grocery shops, and have noticed the ballooning cost of living, much of which can be directly linked to decisions of the administration. Contributing to household belt-tightening are Trump’s deeply unpopular war in Iran, his tariffs on imported goods, the removal of health care cost protections, increased barriers to Medicaid, and his decision to allow the freeze on student loan payments to expire.”

The new survey found that 62 percent of moms think the economic situation will continue to get worse, while 60 percent disapprove of Trump’s handling of the economy. What’s more, 80 percent of mothers say they intend to vote in the midterms, which doesn’t bode well for Republicans, who worry about losing their majority in the House and potentially even the Senate. 

“Although 54 percent of the moms surveyed identified as Republicans, compared with just 43 percent of dads, that partisan advantage did not translate into support for the president,” noted the Daily Beast. “Only 37 percent said they considered themselves primarily Trump supporters. Asked who they voted for in 2024, 50 percent of moms said Donald Trump, with 48 percent nominating Kamala Harris. The drop in support for the president comes as he continues to focus on issues that don’t affect voters or their budgets, but do prop up his ego, like Trump’s obsession with his conspiracy theory that the 2020 election was rigged to ensure he lost.”

Trump’s fallout with moms comes as he’s seen a string of key voter blocs abandon him. In early July, a survey found that he is rapidly losing support among men, who have historically been his most loyal voters. Last month, polls showed that the president is falling behind among the so-called “Reluctant Right” who express they are more likely to vote Democrat in the midterms. Shortly before that, another poll revealed that Trump has dropped to a new low among rural Americans.

news.title: Democrats Dismiss Our Constitutional Traditions As 'Nostalgia'
news.text:
Below is my column in The Hill on the latest spin from the left to convince Americans to abandon core constitutional institutions and values as part of a radical agenda in the upcoming elections. Those who defend our traditions, on the 250th anniversary of our Republic, are now being accused of being “nostalgic” rather than progressive. It is a nostalgia that will take on a truly tragic element if professors, pundits and politicians are successful in this effort.

It appears that the Madisonian democracy has joined shackets and combat boots as embarrassingly outdated for many on the left. In calling for radical changes to our constitutional system, leading Democrats are now calling the lingering loyalty to our traditions as mere “nostalgia.” To be nostalgic in today’s parlance is to be a dupe of the oligarchs and an enemy to reform.

“Nostalgia” has become the new coded term for reactionaries among Democratic figures, who are trying to convince Americans to accept radical changes to our republic after 250 years.

Kamala Harris recently insisted that opposition to ideas like packing the Supreme Court is mere “nostalgia” for a system that is no longer working. “I would caution us against talking about rebuilding with any sense of nostalgia about how things work, because even before, they weren’t working so well for a lot of folks,” she said. That “nostalgia,” according to Harris, is preventing us from doing things like packing the Supreme Court with an instant liberal majority.

California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) last week also declared that “nostalgia is not working” and, while refusing to embrace socialism, added that “capitalism as we know it doesn’t work.”

Morris Katz, a political strategist for Zohran Mamdani, spoke to CNN’s Dana Bash about looking beyond the label of democratic socialism and instead simply to accept that “our government does not work.” They are joining socialists who have long promised revolutionary changes without “introspection, nostalgia or regret.”

It is an all-too-familiar pitch. Sixty years ago, a call to break free of “old ideas, old culture, old customs, and old habits” revolutionized a nation. That call was heard in a Chinese paper that would help lay the foundation for Mao Zedong’s bloody Cultural Revolution. Marxists had long rejected calls to preserve institutions and citizens’ rights as “nostalgia” and “sentimentality,” standing in the way of needed progress.

For the Democratic Socialist of America organization, nostalgia stands in the way of getting rid of the Senate, presidency, and the Supreme Court to fundamentally change the republic.

The effort is to condition Americans to adopt radical changes to our core institutions that professors and pundits say will guarantee Democrats never lose power again.

That is not an easy task for a people who have benefited from the oldest and most prosperous democracy for 250 years. They have to be very angry or very afraid to take such a radical course.

The Soviets understood that about the U.S. After Yuri Bezmenov, a KGB agent working in the media, defected in 1970, he revealed the four stages by which the Soviets hoped to bring about revolutionary change in the U.S. It began with undermining our institutions and values, with the help of journalists and academics.

With establishment figures lining up behind radical changes, including packing the Supreme Court, the public is hearing a constant drumbeat of how our system is broken.

Even Democratic judges are joining the chorus. Indeed, some appear to be auditioning for the slots promised by Democratic leaders to take over the court with a reliable liberal majority.

This week, the Hawaii Supreme Court issued an unhinged diatribe against the U.S. Supreme Court that abandoned any semblance of judicial restraint or decorum. It declared the majority as effectively racists, saying that It portrayed the court as a rogue institution that “overrides what Congress passed. It overrides what the people chose. All to serve its own ends.”

It is an opinion that would make an MS NOW host blush. But it follows a pattern on the left to get people to turn against our institutions and even against the Constitution itself.

Others are telling the public that they are being repressed by the Constitution, which must be scrapped. In a New York Times op-ed — “The Constitution Is Broken and Should Not Be Reclaimed” — law professors Ryan Doerfler of Harvard and Samuel Moyn of Yale called for the nation to “reclaim America from constitutionalism.”

In yet another New York Times editorial, Jennifer Szalai denounced “Constitution worship” and claimed that “Americans have long assumed that the Constitution could save us. A growing chorus now wonders whether we need to be saved from it.”

Berkeley Dean Erwin Chemerinsky insists that it is time to trash the Constitution in favor of “radical changes.”

These voices are seeking to remove all of the moderating elements of our system —the safety features that have produced the world’s most successful and stable republic. They are the very constitutional elements protecting us from the tyranny of the majority, protecting us from ourselves.

Without those protections, we will unleash the self-destructive forces that have been tearing apart other democratic systems since Athens. It is our constitution that spared us from that fate. As James Madison observed, “Had every Athenian citizen been a Socrates, every Athenian assembly would still have been a mob.”

Of course, history has shown that such radical proposals ultimately produce not democracy, but what the Framers called mobocracy. If we let that happen, many Americans will indeed look back at the last 250 years with a tragic sense of nostalgia.

Jonathan Turley is a law professor and the New York Times bestselling author of “Rage and the Republic: The Unfinished Story of the American Revolution.“
news.author: Tyler Durden

news.title: Maine Democrats have found Graham Platner’s replacement. Can he win control of the Senate seat?
news.text:
After the implosion of Graham Platner, many Maine Democrats feared they were lost in the wilderness.

On Monday, it was clear: Troy Jackson is the man they believe will lead them out.

Jackson ended the weekend with the committed support of roughly two-thirds of the delegates in the state who are set to gather this coming weekend and formally nominate a replacement for Platner, whose Senate campaign ended after his support dissolved in the wake of a sexual assault allegation from an ex-girlfriend, which he denied. Three of his rivals for the nomination have already dropped out of the race.

Barring a major shake-up, he’s poised to walk into that convention as the overwhelming favorite.

Now, he has to win the general election against incumbent and Republican Susan Collins - and if he does it could help flip control of the entire Senate to Democrats. 

Jackson’s apparent victory was cheered on by members of the left-wing faction that has challenged numerous Democratic incumbents this cycle and sought to push the party towards a more aggressive populist, working-class centric platform: The Democratic Socialists of America. The DSA, which counts new Democratic primary victors Melat Kiros and Darializa Avila Chevalier among its members, endorsed Jackson as he ran for governor this year in a ranked-choice Democratic primary.

Jackson was defeated in that race, but with the rumors around Platner already swirling by the June primary, one of his supporters presciently suggested on Twitter: “I now want Troy Jackson to not win the governor race and finish 2nd so he’ll most likely be the backup Senate nominee if Platner drops out.”

A DSA-endorsed progressive and former state director for Bernie Sanders’s presidential campaign in 2016, Jackson’s victory represents a major coup for a progressive left wing that took a rhetorical battering in the press after Platner’s withdrawal. 

Accusations of poor vetting and an insufficient focus on the voices of women in the party were leveled at not just Platner but his senior advisers and wider campaign staff, many of whom rallied behind Jackson after the collapse of the Platner bid.

Many of those same progressives were adamant, in the face of that criticism, that Maine voters had spoken clearly and chosen a progressive over the party establishment’s hand-picked candidate, incumbent Maine Gov. Janet Mills, to represent the party in November against Collins. 

Many of Platner’s supporters argued that winning the seat in November would be an impossible and fruitless goal should national Democratic Party figures be allowed to select a candidate to run in his stead.

Barring something major happening, Jackson has seemingly lined up overwhelming support within the state part for his bid. His campaign is now well-positioned to scoop up the existing infrastructure already in place powering Platner’s impressive victory over Mills last month. The question is: Can he recapture the same voter enthusiasm that accompanied Platner’s win?

On paper, Jackson checks all the boxes. As his endorsements (which also included Bernie Sanders in his gubernatorial bid, and the Maine AFL-CIO) would suggest, Jackson is a dyed-in-the-wool progressive, supporting Medicare for All, the abolition of Immigration and Customs Enforcement and an end to American support of Israel’s assault on Gaza, which he’s referred to as a genocide. 

Like Platner, he also hails from a working-class background, having previously worked as a logger before joining Maine’s state senate, and later serving as its majority leader.

Whether Jackson can translate his similarities into the same momentum against Collins is an open question, though, as he came in third place in the Maine gubernatorial primary this year despite his list of powerful endorsements. Supporters argued that he built an impressive ground game and fared well against politicians with better name recognition, but some are still skeptical about his path forward. 

Some fans of Platner and other Democratic observers questioned whether Jackson had the same charisma that led Platner to pack town hall events to capacity for months ahead of his primary victory in June.

Collins, meanwhile, has nearly faded into the background amid her onetime opponent’s self-destruction. 

Much as the Trump campaign did in 2024, Collins’s campaign has largely stayed out of the fray as Democrats rushed to replace Platner — the incumbent senator, 73, is taking a more passive approach to campaigning as she holds on to advantages in name recognition, her reputation as a moderate in the Senate and a massive war chest supported by Senate GOP leadership which is waiting to flood the airwaves with more than $100 million in reserved advertising space through November.

The reality for Jackson is stark: His quick collection of the delegates necessary to win the Senate nomination was only the first battle he’ll face, with a much tougher one coming down the pipes. 

He’s stepping into a race where the comparisons to Kamala Harris’s doomed 2024 bid are widely apparent, banking on the notion that a unified Democratic Party and dissatisfaction for the Trump administration and the Republican Congress will be enough to take a jump-started campaign over the finish line against potentially the hardest target for Democrats to beat of this entire cycle.
news.summary: Graham Platner’s self-destruction was a major blow to progressives, but his replacement proves that their critics don’t have much juice, writes John Bowden
news.author: John Bowden

news.title: Things Just Got So Much Worse for the Democrats
news.text:
I previously reported that Democrats are squandering a golden opportunity in this year’s midterms. Even though history is on their side, and Trump's approval numbers are underwater, the party keeps choosing to obsess over Trump and stand idly by as its socialist wing takes over. The result is a generic ballot lead so thin it wouldn't cover a bar tab, nowhere near what analysts say Democrats need to flip the House.

Now let's kick them while they're down.

The Republican National Committee closed out June with $128.5 million in cash on hand. That's not just good. That's the highest month-end total in the history of the party. The RNC pulled in another $16.7 million in June alone, stacking on top of the $125.5 million it had in May and the $123.9 million it had in April. Three months. Three records.

Are you seeing a pattern here?

RNC Chairman Joe Gruters isn't shy about who deserves the credit. "United, disciplined, and on offense," is how he described Republicans in a statement to Newsmax, crediting President Donald Trump and Vice President JD Vance for handing the party the largest war chest in its history heading into the midterms. Gruters made clear the RNC plans to spend nearly all of it defending the House and Senate majorities.

Related: Here's How the Democrats Are Blowing the Midterms

And here's the part that should really sting for Democrats: Gruters didn't have to invent a contrast. He just described what's happening. While Republicans are unified and flush with cash, Democrats are dealing with internal divisions and fundraising headaches in race after race.

And they’re deep in debt. The RNC, however, has zero debt.

Now, of course, money isn’t everything. Just ask Hillary Clinton and Kamala Harris. But there’s no denying that the RNC’s money advantage will be critical. In fact, the RNC has already deployed 34 state directors and election integrity directors across 17 battleground states. Vance, the first sitting vice president to chair the RNC's finance operation, has been working both ends of the donor spectrum, courting big checks while keeping the grassroots money flowing too.

It doesn't stop at the RNC. The Senate Leadership Fund and its nonprofit partner, One Nation, raised $140 million in the second quarter and finished June with $238 million in the bank, nearly double what they had at this point in the 2024 cycle. That money is earmarked for defending Republican Senate seats while going after Democratic incumbents in Georgia, Michigan, and New Hampshire.

House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) is doing his part too. He raised $19.1 million in the second quarter, pushing his 2026 total past $53 million, and he's been funneling that cash straight to vulnerable incumbents and challengers trying to protect the House majority.

Add it up, and the picture is brutal for Democrats. Republicans are entering the final stretch united, debt-free, and sitting on more money than at any point in party history, while Democrats are struggling with debt and a socialist insurgency. With fewer than four months left until Election Day, the gap isn't just in the polling. It's in the bank accounts. Make no mistake about it, money doesn't win elections by itself, but Democrats are managing to lose on every front at once, and that takes real effort.

Editor’s Note: The 2026 Midterms will determine the fate of President Trump’s America First agenda. Republicans must maintain control of both chambers of Congress.

news.title: Troy Jackson surges as Maine Democrats’ likely Senate pick after Graham Platner's exit
news.text:
Troy Jackson, a former Maine lawmaker and fifth-generation logger from the North Maine Woods, has emerged as the likely new replacement for the state’s Democratic Senate nominee after former candidate Graham Platner quit the high-stakes race less than two weeks ago.

Platner’s departure followed a sexual assault allegation that he denied but forced his exit anyway. It sparked a wave of potential candidates eager to prove to Mainers that they could take on longtime Republican Sen. Susan Collins.

But by Sunday, the field had largely been cleared after Jackson supporters spent the weekend securing hundreds of delegates who promised to back him at the upcoming state convention. Under a never-before-used system to replace Platner, delegates will vote Saturday in Bangor on a new Senate nominee. Out of the 601 delegates who will gather this weekend, Jackson’s campaign says they have 481 delegates committed to supporting him.

Top opponents — ranging from Secretary of State Shenna Bellows to Nirav Shah, former director of Maine’s Center for Disease Control and Prevention — have since dropped out, while Jackson announced Monday that he was focusing his attention on ousting Collins.

“I’m grateful to Democrats across Maine for putting their faith in me and in what we’re fighting for,” Jackson said in a statement. “This didn’t happen because of one person. It happened because hundreds of volunteers put in the hours, knocked on doors, made phone calls, and believed we could build something bigger than ourselves.”

There are challenges ahead for Jackson in a race with Collins

Yet securing the nomination is only just the beginning of challenges facing Jackson. There’s little more than 100 days until the general election, meaning Jackson faces an uphill battle raising campaign funds when Collins already has millions. Jackson also must implement a statewide campaign network and woo over the state’s large independent base.

As former state Senate president, Jackson may be more politically vetted than Platner was running as a first-time candidate, but the logger’s backing of progressive policies will leave him open to plenty of attacks by Republicans, said Nicholas Jacobs, a political scientist at Colby College in Maine.

“The guy does have some pretty interesting positions,” Jacobs said. “Positions that are pretty far to the left of the median voter.”

Jackson supports ‘Medicare for All’ and eliminating ICE

Like Platner, Jackson has said he wants to “get rid of” Immigration and Customs Enforcement, which has become top of mind for many Mainers after an ICE officer fatally shot a driver last week. Democrats have rushed to connect Collins to the embattled federal agency, while Jackson has reiterated his support of dissolving ICE altogether.

Jackson has also called for “Medicare for All,” which would replace job-based and individual private health insurance with a government-run plan that guarantees coverage for all with no premiums, no deductibles and only minimal copays for certain services.

Republicans were already criticizing Jackson as a “socialist” on Monday.

“This entire process is just as bad as Kamala’s rigged coronation,” said Republican National Committee spokesperson Kristen Cianci, nodding to former President Joe Biden’s decision to drop out of the presidential race in July 2024, leaving Vice President Kamala Harris to head up the Democratic ticket and ultimately lose to Republican Donald Trump.

“Maine voters have had their voices stolen by party insiders,” Cianci added.

Jackson first ran for office as a Republican

However, unlike the scrutiny Platner faced from a series of controversies, Jackson’s background coming from “old Maine” and his decades of working in the logging industry will help him, Jacobs said.

“He’s the real deal,” Jacobs said.

In 1998, Jackson helped lead a weeklong blockade along the Quebec border in an effort to protest the hiring of Canadian loggers who were coming into Maine and driving down pay rates. The standoff ended with Jackson being escorted away by police, but resulted in Jackson fighting for better logger and wood hauler protections as a state lawmaker.

Jackson initially ran as a Republican in 2000 when he sought a seat inside Maine’s House of Representatives. He lost, but won two years later when he ran as an independent. He switched to the Democratic Party in 2004, and eventually became the Maine Senate president before leaving the Statehouse in 2024.

He came in third while running for governor in this year’s Democratic primary election, but had the backing of Platner, U.S. Sen. Bernie Sanders and Our Revolution, the political organization founded by Sanders.

Democrats must net four Senate seats to gain control of the 100-member chamber, and party leaders viewed Maine as a critical piece of the puzzle, along with Alaska, Ohio and North Carolina.
news.summary: Troy Jackson, a former Maine lawmaker and fifth-generation logger from the North Maine Woods, has emerged as the likely new replacement for the state’s Democratic Senate nominee after former candidate Graham Platner quit the high-stakes race less than two weeks…

news.title: Who is Troy Jackson, Democrats' likely Maine Senate nominee?
news.text:
