news.summary: Troy Jackson has effectively secured the Democratic nomination for Maine's Senate race after winning support from more than two thirds of the state's delegates, with rivals Shenna Bellows and Nirav Shah dropping out.
news.author: Sagar Kar

news.title: Troy Jackson dominates Maine Democratic caucuses, emerges as frontrunner to face Susan Collins
news.text:
Following Platner’s drop-out over allegations that he raped a girlfriend five years ago, Jackson sought to distance himself from Platner personally while hugging the grassroots base that had elevated Platner to win the primary with 72 percent of the vote and outlast a two-term incumbent governor. 

Jackson, meanwhile, finished third in the Democratic primary for governor on the same day.

Jackson clearly wasn’t penalized by activists for his previous link with Planter. In fact, he so dominated county caucuses on Saturday and Sunday that his top two rivals — Secretary of State Shenna Bellows and Nirav Shah, former director of the Maine Center for Disease Control and Prevention — both ended their campaigns. Dan Kleban, co-founder of Maine Beer Company, also dropped his bid and endorsed Jackson. 

In 3 of the state’s 16 counties, Jackson’s supporters won every delegate spot. In five others, he won all but one delegate. That included Cumberland County, the state’s largest by population, where Jackson won 148 out of 149 delegates, with the lone outlier uncommitted.

In total, Jackson amassed 456 out of the 500 elected delegates over the weekend. When added to the 101 existing state committee members who vote, that is well over the 302 needed to secure the nomination at next Saturday’s convention.

If the results seemed swift, it was partly out of necessity and partly due to Jackson’s ability to organize supporters quickly. Maine state law only gave Democrats two weeks to name a replacement. While the convention will formally do so two days before the deadline, Democrats appear to have pulled off the entire process in 12 days— unless something unforeseen happens in the next week.

Jordan Wood, who lost a congressional primary last month, stressed in an interview with the Globe on Sunday that he was vowing to stay in the contest through the convention. According to a Bangor Daily News tabulation, he had only two supporters elected as delegates over the weekend.

It is unclear whether that remains a tenable position for Wood. It is also unclear whether CNN and the Bangor Daily News will proceed with their joint debate on Thursday night, or whether Jackson would even bother participating if they do.

The caucuses themselves were remarkably efficient affairs. Over 17,000 registered Democrats voted in their home county caucuses, according to state party officials. After being verified, each voter received an email and text message with a unique web link to their ballot.

At York County’s caucus on Sunday morning, hundreds filled the Sanford High School theater. For two hours, Democrats mingled, gathered, and checked in at their preferred candidate’s campaign table, if one was set up. Jackson rallied his supporters outside. Then, within 10 minutes of the formal meeting starting, voting got underway without any speeches from the campaigns.

The more organized campaigns, like Jackson’s and Shah’s, offered supporters shirts to wear (dark blue for Jackson, green for Shah) and handed out a slate of campaign-aligned delegates to vote for on the ballot.

This slate-of-delegates approach appeared helpful to voters looking to support a specific Senate campaign. In York County, for example, 444 candidates ran for 84 delegate and 17 alternate delegate positions. Each candidate was listed by name, with no indication of whom they supported. Due to the sheer number of votes to be cast, voters were given 90 minutes to complete their ballots.

Voters could be seen holding a piece of paper with their preferred campaign’s delegate selections in one hand while casting votes on their phones with the other.

As voting began, a line of a few dozen people seeking technical help from party staff and volunteers slowly dissipated after 20 minutes. County party officials noted that many voters stayed home and cast their ballots via phone or computer.

Jackson’s dominant performance will likely pressure the remaining candidates to leave the race to make unifying the party easier ahead of Saturday’s convention.

The snap election process across the state — driven largely by party insiders and activists — appeared to give a significant leg up to candidates who ran for governor a month ago, like Jackson, who had already built statewide organizations over the past year.

As Jackson likely spends the next week trying to unite Democrats behind him and quickly scale up a Senate campaign with roughly 100 days until Election Day, one immediate concern is fundraising. Jackson raised the least money among the major candidates for governor this year.

Republican groups backing Collins have already spent or reserved over $112 million in ads, while Democrats have reserved only half that amount, according to tracking service AdImpact. Given Maine’s critical role in deciding control of the U.S. Senate next year, however, Democrats nationwide could flood the state with funds.

For Democrats to flip the Senate, they must accomplish two goals: win competitive elections in states currently held by Democrats -- namely New Hampshire, Michigan, and Georgia -- and flip four states currently held by Republicans. Maine is the only state with a current Republican senator up for election this year in a state that Kamala Harris won in 2024.

“We know the nation has been watching this wild process up here, but it seems to be coming to a quick conclusion in a way that surprised me,” said Ethan Standel, a 32-year-old Jackson supporter from Saco who voted in person on Sunday.

James Pindell is a Globe political reporter who reports and analyzes American politics, especially in New England.
news.author: James Pindell Globe

news.title: AOC accused of putting on new accent during Memphis rally: 'Going full Kamala'
news.text: Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., was accused of putting on an accent during a Memphis rally last week, with critics on social media comparing her to former Vice President Kamala Harris. Ocasio-Cortez was speaking at a campaign rally in Memphis on Friday for progressive U.S. House candidate Justin Pearson, who currently serves as a Tennessee state representative. "We cannot afford to leave Tennessee behind," she said. "We cannot afford to leave Kentucky behind, Alabama behind, Mississippi behind, Georgia behind, South Carolina behind. There is no country where we are split apart." Ayanna] Pressley and I were in Montgomery, Alabama, and one of the things that we said was that it is time for the North to roll up to the South," Ocasio-Cortez continued. "AOC, from the ‘Bronx’ sounding like a southern Baptist preacher while in Memphis," actor Michael Rapaport, who has said he’ll run for the New York City mayor’s office in 2029, posted on social media. news.title: Auschwitz survivor, 96, in NYC forced to hide Star of David as he gives Dems history lesson on antisemitism
news.text:
It’s a dire case of deja vu!

A 96-year-old Auschwitz survivor said he now hides his Star of David chain because of spiraling antisemitism in the Big Apple as he gave Democrats a brutal history lesson about anti-Jewish hatred.

Jerry Wartski said the Democratic Party and the liberal Democratic Socialists of America are being infiltrated by antisemites who are fueling an environment that has Jews feeling unsafe in the streets as if it were Europe during the rise of the Third Reich.

“You have to keep your eyes open to what’s going on,” Wartski told The Post in a recent interview. “I cover up my Star of David chain under my shirt when I walk outside now.

“It’s the same old story,” said Wartski, whose parents were killed in concentration camps during the Nazis’ systemic slaughtering of Jews in the Holocaust. “I close the shirt. I don’t advertise. This is the `Land of the Free, Home of the Brave?'”

Wartski said he’s been growing uneasy over what he feels is a tangible change in New York and the US, which were once safe havens for Jews who fled the Nazis and oppression in other countries.

Now, Jews could face hostility here or abroad, he added.

“If you run away from New York, you run away from the United States. There’s nowhere else to go,” he said. “Europe is corroded.”

Poland-born Wartski and an older brother survived the Nazi wipeout of two ghettos — his hometown Ozorkow and Lodz — before his family was shipped to Auschwitz, where his mother was gassed to death when he was still a child, he said.

Later, he was relocated to Nordhausen concentration camp in Germany, where his father died just before the American liberation.

Today, he has grown disillusioned with the Democratic Party because it appears to be succumbing to anti-Jewish rhetoric under what he said is a coalition of socialists, communists and radical Islamists.

Wartski blasted democratic socialist Mayor Zohran Mamdani, a staunch pro-Palestinian activist and outspoken critic of Israel, as part of the problem.

“Mamdani is definitely an antisemite — a big one. He doesn’t hide it.” Watski said. “I didn’t vote for him.”

Mamdani, the city’s first Muslim mayor, backs the controversial boycott, divestment and sanctions campaign against Israel, which some view as antisemitic.

Wartski backs President Trump, saying he’s fighting the good fight against the radical Islamists in Iran and supports Republican Bruce Blakeman for governor.

During a Post interview in 2024, he criticized Democrat Kamala Harris for likening Trump to Hitler.

The Holocaust survivor said despite his worries about the state of the city today, he’s lived a good life in New York, running his own sweater-knitting business before launching a career in real estate.

“I was lucky during the war. I was lucky in business. I survived,” he said.
news.summary: He's seen the evil of antisemitism before -- and he's seeing it again! Jerry Wartski, a 96-year-old Holocaust survivor, said he now hides his Star of David chain when he walks New York City's streets because of Jew hatred.
news.author: Carl Campanile

news.title: Courthouse weddings are booming as many couples skip pricey traditional ceremonies
news.text: A number of West Coast couples are reportedly foregoing costly, extravagant weddings these days. Instead, they're favoring simple courthouse ceremonies. The demand for straightforward civil ceremonies is causing a rush for marriage appointments at municipal buildings in California, the Los Angeles Times recently reported. "They pretty much get picked up as soon as we release them," Santa Barbara County Clerk Melinda Greene told the publication. "We have people from all over the world." TAYLOR SWIFT'S WEDDING SPARKS HOLIDAY WEEKEND ETIQUETTE DEBATE FOR GUESTS Slots to get married at California courthouses have been filling up months in advance, while some couples snap up last-minute bookings. Elopement has turned from "a breezy wedding alternative into a formal contact sport," the same publication reported. "It books up so fast, you have to be online at the exact time [of day] you plan on having your appointment," Asha Marshall of So Fetch Photography told the Times. "A lot of my brides get stressed out." Micro-weddings are reportedly on the rise due in part to social media posts inspiring couples to get married at the same venues where celebrities have tied the knot. Santa Barbara’s Spanish Moorish revival courthouse, for instance, is a sought-after spot where Kamala Harris married Doug Emhoff and where Kourtney Kardashian got hitched to Travis Barker. TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ "We see dozens a day, starting at 8 o’clock in the morning," Roy Lee, Santa Barbara County supervisor, told the Times. San Francisco City Hall, where Marilyn Monroe and Joe DiMaggio were married with great fanfare in 1954, is a top spot for a courthouse wedding, according to the Times. The venue hosts some 7,000 weddings a year. The trend also coincides with Gen Z — those commonly cited as having been born between 1997 and 2012 — now entering its peak marrying years. The oldest members of Gen Z are hitting the average marrying age of 28, online wedding-planning and registry company Zola reported, and this generation has become a primary driver of the wedding industry. Micro-weddings are reportedly on the rise, due in part to social media. "The 2026 wedding season reflects the broader cultural shifts of this generation," Zola reported in its "2026 Wedding Trends: First Look" report, a survey of more than 11,500 couples getting married in 2026. CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS "Our couples are using TikTok and AI in their everyday lives, embracing both cocktails and mocktails and prioritizing their mental wellness," the report said. Though cost-friendly and streamlined civil marriage ceremonies are popular in some places, the average cost of a wedding is $36,000, according to Zola — while the average guest count has remained steady at around 145 people, similar to last year. "Even with economic jitters, we’re seeing couples prioritize the celebration they want over other financial goals," the company reported. "The sentiment is clear: 85% of couples say their wedding will be worth every penny." news.title: AOC Deploys New Attempt At Southern Charm During Tennessee Campaign Stop
news.text:
Democratic New York Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez broke out a faux Southern accent during a Saturday campaign appearance in Tennessee.

Ocasio-Cortez, seen as a likely challenger for either the 2028 Democratic nomination for president or for the Senate seat held by Democratic New York Sen. Chuck Schumer, accentuated her remarks while campaigning for Democratic Tennessee state Rep. Justin Pearson. Pearson is running for the seat in the U.S. House of Representatives currently held by retiring Democratic Rep. Steve Cohen. Ocasio-Cortez urged Pearson’s supporters to not write off the state, which favors Republicans, while attempting to evoke the dynacism of a preacher. (RELATED: CNN Panel Melts Down After Kevin O’Leary Compares AOC’s Munich Word Salad To Kamala Harris)

AOC, from the “Bronx” sounding like a southern Baptist preacher while in Memphis. pic.twitter.com/TEHd6HbC3X— MichaelRapaport (@MichaelRapaport) July 18, 2026

“We cannot afford to leave Tennessee behind,” Ocasio-Cortez, who was joined by Democratic Massachusetts Rep. Ayanna Pressley, said. “We cannot afford to leave Kentucky behind, Alabama behind, Mississippi behind, Georgia behind, South Carolina behind.”

Ocasio-Cortez’s attempted accent appears similar to the variety of accents then-Vice President Kamala Harris deployed while campaigning for president in 2024 as the Democratic nominee. At one point, Harris used two different accents during separate campaign stops on the same day.

AOC adopted a lower profile after a disastrous performance during a February security conference in Munich when asked about American policy on Taiwan. The democratic socialist lawmaker rambled for 40 seconds about whether the U.S. should send troops to defend the democratic Republic of China if the communist People’s Republic of China attacked the island.

The left-wing Democrat, one of the more famous members of “The Squad,” also repeated comments about the American northern states needing to “roll up to the South” that she drew fire for after making them during a May political appearance in Montgomery, Ala.

AOC: “It is time for the North to roll up to the South.”What? pic.twitter.com/wLsOHfdg9Z— RNC Research (@RNCResearch) July 18, 2026

“We will not be chased out of places anymore, brothers and sisters,” Ocasio-Cortez said. “It’s going take all us together rejecting separation, for us organize and speak life into one another because we will, we will see the day where we will turn the crooked path straight.”
news.summary: ‘Roll up to the South’
news.author: Democratic New York,Harold Hutchison

news.title: New poll reveals what Americans really think of MAGA and it could be bad news for Trump
news.text:
The midterm elections are coming up, but just how MAGA fans feel about their current sitting president and founder of the movement - Donald J Trump – has been revealed. 

Just two days ago it was announced that Trump had joined predecessors Joe Biden and Jimmy Carter with his latest approval rating reveal on inflation, but what about his overall rating? 

The latest inflation approval ratings found Trump's net approval rating on inflation improved slightly to minus-47 points in June, from minus-50 points in May. 

However, when it comes to looking at Trump's popularity, there's a lot of other pieces at play. 

With just over 100 days until the midterm elections, the CNBC All-America Economic Survey showed just what voters think of the job the administration is doing, and if this bodes well for Trump's term. 

The survey polled 1,000 voters from July 8 to July 12 and painted a bleak picture of the likelihood of MAGA being voted for again by previous supporters. 

That's because the poll found that 57 per cent of respondents said they are unlikely to support any self-identified MAGA candidates this year. 

The poll also found 61 per cent of the group feel pessimistic about the current state of the American economy and its future too. 

Despite these numbers, the POTUS has been vocal about his belief that his approval ratings are his highest 'ever', but his rating has been through the ringer, which seems to be caused in part by the US and Israeli a war with Iran. 

Focaldata conducted a poll released on July 6, and reported Trump's approval rating sat at minus 23, which isn't exactly what you'd call the 'highest ever'. 

A CBS News/YouGov poll in May also revealed that there has been a decrease in voter confidence. 

60 per cent of those polled claimed they are now disapproving of Trump’s handling of his job in the White House. 

Then, 54 per cent of white non-college voters confirmed they too disapproved of Trump’s performance, with 61 per cent being unhappy with the work he is doing, and 66 per cent citing the Iran war as being a bad choice. 

The midterm elections are held halfway through a president's four-year term, and are scheduled to commence on November 3. 

In light of the date coming up, former Democratic presidential candidate, Kamala Harris has urged voters to use their vote with determination. 

She claimed Trump is 'scared of your power' and that he 'wants you to believe that your vote does not matter'. 

"He wants you to lose confidence in our electoral system so you stay home this November," she continued in a statement, noting: "He knows how discontent the American people are, and he wants to make sure that you do not vote." 

She told the people: "Because if the people vote in their numbers, Republicans will lose." 

In a final push to get the public to vote to end Trump's reign, she said to make their 'voices heard' and 'put a real check on this corrupt and cruel administration'. 

UNILAD reached out to the White House for comment.
news.summary: Donald Trump previously claimed to have the 'highest' approval rating 'ever' in a Truth Social post
news.author: Britt Jones

news.title: Despite Trump's unpopularity, new poll shows tight race for Congress
news.text:
As President Donald Trump faces near-record-low disapproval ratings, a new Washington Post-Ipsos poll, conducted July 8-13, found that Democrats have a slim advantage over Republicans ahead of November's midterm elections.

Still, Democratic supporters are more motivated to vote. The WaPo-Ipsos poll shows Democrats leading Republicans by 3 percentage points, 48% to 45%, two years after Kamala Harris' 2024 loss to Trump.

The poll also found that Democrats' edge grows to 8 points among those "absolutely certain to vote" as 53% of highly motivated voters support Democrats while 45% support Republicans. Trump has faced low approval numbers amid issues with the economy and the Iran war.

The Post-Ipsos survey contrasted Democrats' current polling outcomes with 2018. Democrats had a 10-point lead in support for Congress ahead of his first presidential midterm election in which Democrats gained 40 U.S. House seats. Republicans maintained their majority in the Senate.

Trump continues to face waning approval numbers. His overall approval rating stands at 37 percent in the WaPo-Ipsos poll. A new Economist/YouGov poll, conducted July 10-13, found that the same number of Americans approve of his job performance.

What Trump family members will attend the FIFA World Cup Final?

House Speaker Mike Johnson currently has a two-vote margin and cannot afford defections as Republicans hope to enact Trump's agenda. Republicans currently have a 53-47 seat majority in the Senate. Partisan gerrymandering has thrust further uncertainty into Democrats' hopes to regain control of both chambers.

The poll found that Democrats are 10% more likely to certainly vote this year than Republicans, which eclipses their two-point edge in 2018.

Contributing: Fernando Cervantes Jr., Zachary Schermele
news.author: Usa Today

news.title: Gad Saad, the doctor who diagnosed the West
news.text:
The pattern kept repeating itself elsewhere. The same activists who were championing transgender athletes seemed to have no empathy left over for the female athletes that trans athletes were displacing, sometimes dangerously. The same voices demanding that we welcome Muslim immigrants with open arms had, for years, precious little to say about the mostly working-class girls who were being terrorized by Pakistani Muslim rape gangs in Britain on an industrial scale. (I also found it extremely disturbing how the same legacy media that couldn’t get enough of the Catholic Church sexual abuse story during the early 2000s have been virtually silent about the Pakistani Muslim sexual abuse story today. The chances that we ever get a Hollywood-produced movie about the United Kingdom’s “grooming” gangs, à la Spotlight, are next to nil.) The very people who pride themselves on defending the vulnerable were somehow managing to see no evil and hear no evil when one of the worst evils imaginable was being inflicted upon one of the most vulnerable groups of people in our society.

It was somewhere in the middle of watching all this that I found the vocabulary for what I’d been sensing. That vocabulary is the product of the mind of Dr. Gad Saad, and it’s the subject of his essential new book, Suicidal Empathy: Dying to Be Kind, which debuted at No. 1 on the New York Times bestseller list.

I had interviewed Saad once before, on the occasion of his 2023 book, The Saad Truth About Happiness: 8 Secrets for Leading the Good Life. That conversation left me with a clear impression: Here was one of the sharpest, wittiest, and most engaging public intellectuals working today — someone who could move from hard behavioral science to cultural commentary to outright comedy in the space of a single sentence. That impression is what sent me back to him for this book, on this subject. I suspected he’d have something worthwhile to say. He did — considerably more than that, actually.

Who is Gad Saad?

Saad was a marketing professor at Concordia University in Montreal from 1994 to 2026, where he held the Concordia University Research Chair in Evolutionary Behavioral Sciences and Darwinian Consumption from 2008 to 2018. He has held visiting professorships at Cornell University, Dartmouth College, the University of California-Irvine, and Northwood University, and is currently in the process of moving to the United States, where, as of this August, he’ll be joining the University of Mississippi as distinguished visiting professor of the school’s Declaration of Independence Center for the Study of American Freedom. (This past year, he was a scholar at the Declaration of Independence Center while on leave from his Concordia University professorship.) Over more than three decades in academia, he has authored more than 75 scientific papers, pioneering the application of evolutionary psychology to marketing and consumer behavior — a career that has earned him, among other honors, the 2015 President’s Media Outreach Award as Concordia’s top global research communicator.

But it’s Saad’s public-facing work that has made him a household name well beyond the academy. His Psychology Today column, “Homo Consumericus,” has amassed over 7.47 million reads across more than 300 pieces. His YouTube show and podcast, The Saad Truth, have racked up more than 50 million views and downloads. His following spans an unusually wide ideological and cultural range — Elon Musk, Joe Rogan, Jordan Peterson, and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi have all counted themselves among his admirers.

That crossover appeal is not an accident. Saad’s 2020 book, The Parasitic Mind: How Infectious Ideas Are Killing Common Sense, became an international bestseller by diagnosing the West’s susceptibility to what he called “idea pathogens” — bad ideas that spread virally and hijack sound reasoning. Suicidal Empathy is, in a sense, the sequel and the sharpening: a follow-on diagnosis of one specific idea pathogen that Saad now regards as the master key to understanding nearly everything currently going wrong in Western societies.

A book, a term, and a cultural moment

Suicidal Empathy argues that a corrupted, dysregulated form of compassion — empathy that “hyperfires” toward the wrong targets, in Saad’s phrase from our conversation — now drives a staggering share of the West’s worst policy decisions. The book traces the anatomy of the affliction across eight chapters, from “A Good Virtue Gone Bad” through “Blank Slate Felons” to a closing prescription, “Inoculation Against Suicidal Empathy.”

Saad doesn’t pull any punches; he’s pugilistic in the best sense of the term: intellectually and comedically. The book is laced with the kind of caustic wit that has become his signature. He describes reflexive reality-denial as “Ostrich Parasitic Syndrome” (a term originating in Chapter 7 of his The Parasitic Mind). He calls those most thoroughly captured by the affliction “invertebrate castrati.” In one memorably unsparing aside, he describes Kamala Harris as a “lobotomized cackler.” Saad likens the suicidally empathetic to Gregor Samsa’s transformation in Kafka’s The Metamorphosis — people, he writes, who have undergone “a new form of human-to-insect metamorphosis.” I’d add my own Kafka comparison: The suicidally empathetic of the West have been behaving like the title character of “A Hunger Artist,” who starves himself to death performatively, publicly, and entirely unnecessarily. When his lack of appetite is literally killing him, at that point, the only sane thing to do would be to at least try to eat something, anything. As a Jewish mother would say, Force yourself!

The book catalogs the symptoms with a satirist’s eye for the absurd: Dieting and simply not being overweight has been recast as “white supremacy”; how President Joe Biden publicly regretted calling the man who murdered Laken Riley an “illegal” rather than “undocumented”; how the “DIE (Diversity, Inclusion, and Equity) cult” has metastasized to the point where once-fine schools like the University of Michigan are now spending tens of millions of dollars a year on DEI positions.

“Imagine the number of leading professors who could have been hired with that wasted money,” Saad writes. Or imagine how much more affordable universities could make higher education if they stopped paying these “indulgences” (as Saad describes them) to the high priests of Orthodox Wokeism and instead refocused their efforts and resources on actual education.

Saad is particularly incisive on the West’s asymmetric protectiveness. “The West’s reflex,” he writes, “is always to protect Islam irrespective of the unfolding realities.” Why does one specific religion receive this reflexive deference that others do not? Within the mysterious theology of Wokeism, why is it that some religions are more equal than others?

Saad is equally incisive on the linguistic inanities that suicidal empathy uses to camouflage its deleterious, often violent consequences. These are terms that are so Orwellian that they might even shock Orwell himself.

“Gender-affirming care,” Saad notes, launders what is in fact life-altering, often irreversible medical intervention into something anodyne, making one forget the enormous violence that one is inflicting upon oneself—sacrificing one’s ability to reproduce, and thereby killing any of one’s potential future children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren — by undergoing such a procedure. (In that same spirit, I’d nominate “reproductive rights” as another entrant in the genre — a phrase built to obscure the fact that it is authorizing the murder of potential human life.)

And on the wokeist hierarchy that ranks victim groups against one another, Saad paraphrases Orwell’s Animal Farm: “All lives are important but some a lot more so than others.” That same hierarchy produces demands as arbitrary as insisting that a film adaptation of The Odyssey feature more trans actors — a demand no more coherent, he notes, than insisting that the Dallas Mavericks have more Lebanese Jews on their roster.

A unified field theory

What elevates Suicidal Empathy above a mere grievance-cataloging exercise, and what I asked Saad about directly, is its ambition to function as something like a unified field theory in physics: one underlying mechanism that explains a sprawling, seemingly unconnected list of contemporary pathologies, from DEI to open borders to the collapse of common sense around crime and public safety.

I asked Saad whether he had a Eureka! moment in which he came up with the term (and the diagnosis) of “suicidal empathy.” The term’s roots, he told me, trace back further than most readers realize. He used the term in The Parasitic Mind, but its true origin lies even earlier: in the transcript of his testimony before the Canadian Senate on Bill C-16, where he first flagged what he called the miscalibration of empathy. The conceptual scaffolding came from his years in which he was immersed in the work of E.O. Wilson, particularly Wilson’s writing on why communism fails as a human system despite its appeal — “great idea, wrong species,” as Wilson pithily put it — and from Wilson’s book Consilience, a term, Saad noted, that “many people don’t have in their lexicon.” Having spent decades absorbing sociobiology’s central premise — that biology shapes behavior across every species, humans included — Saad found himself, as he put it, hard-wired to look for synthesis. Confronted with DEI, BLM, open-borders policy, calls to “defund the police,” and more, he went looking for the single mechanism beneath all of them, and suicidal empathy is what he found.

That synthesis, he argues, is driven at root by misplaced guilt and what he calls “collective impostor syndrome,” a kind of civilizational self-doubt that twists compassion into self-destruction.

“The West is engaging in civilizational seppuku,” Saad told me, and he was insistent that the framing isn’t merely rhetorical. “This is not just phraseology.”

The Western mind, in his account, has been “hijacked by parasitic thinking” in a way that causes the empathy instinct to fire reflexively toward the wrong. Saad was candid that different societies sit at different points along this trajectory. Much of Europe, he believes, is far further gone than the United States. America benefits, he told me, from “a set of built-in inoculations,” chiefly our First Amendment, that have slowed the disease’s progress here. “But that doesn’t mean the U.S. is inoculated from it,” he cautioned.

Saad pointed to Dearborn, Michigan, as an early marker, with Paterson, New Jersey, potentially next, and Minnesota after that. Extrapolate a century forward, he suggested, and the number of American Dearborns and Patersons could multiply considerably. There are some grounds for optimism, he noted, that this trajectory can still be reversed — but he was blunt about the obstacles: “I see no evidence that the West is willing to implement” the corrective measures that would actually be required to root out the mental parasite that is suicidal empathy.

As for when the parasite first took hold, Saad traces a genealogy stretching back roughly a century, to the rise of cultural relativism, with postmodernism entering the bloodstream about 50 years ago, and other contributing strains arriving more recently — a constellation of factors that had to align before suicidal empathy could metastasize into the dominant force in Western societies that Saad believes it has become.

The book and the term are already having a major impact on political and sociocultural discourse. Elon Musk has been enthusiastically promoting the book on X (the book bears his endorsement as well) in posts that reach tens of millions of people. The term has already migrated into political discourse abroad — Saad cited its uptake among politicians, political pundits, and cultural commentators.

Toward the end of our conversation, Saad lamented how our society has fallen to such a state where books such as Suicidal Empathy have become necessary. It is, he said, “so regrettable that it even requires me to write these books,” referring to both this one and The Parasitic Mind before it. As he put it, all of the problems that these books seek to address “have been self-inflicted.”

It’s a fitting image, and one I’d extend a bit further: It’s rather like being treated in a hospital for self-inflicted wounds. We can thank the doctor profusely for his skill in stitching us back up, but we’re the ones who did the cutting. Ideally, we’d never have cut ourselves at all. Since we have, we should be grateful that a medic as perspicacious as Saad is in the emergency room to treat the wound. It now falls to the rest of us, our institutions and our politicians included, to make sure we stop inflicting new ones.

Infinite tolerance or survival?

This past academic year, I served as a visiting scholar at Rutgers University, where I taught courses on the history of antisemitism. Saad’s book, and the framework it offers, would sit comfortably on that syllabus if I were to teach the course again. It isn’t just that Suicidal Empathy explains the specific asymmetry I kept encountering — sympathy for black victims but silence for Jewish ones, solidarity with trans athletes but indifference toward the women sidelined by them, protectiveness toward Muslim immigrant communities but willful blindness toward the girls victimized by Pakistani rape gangs. It’s that Saad’s book supplies the single mechanism underlying it all: a wokeist hierarchy in which, per his Orwellian paraphrase, some people really are more equal than others.

Saad closes his book with a line that is destined to outlast the news cycle that produced it: “A society dies when it cares more about exhibiting infinite tolerance and empathy than invoking its survival instinct. It truly is that simple.”

Daniel Ross Goodman (@DanRossGoodman) is a Washington Examiner contributing writer and teaches theology and religious studies at St. John’s University. His next book, Dante’s Guide to Life: How The Divine Comedy Can Change Our Fortunes, Our World, and Ourselves, will be published this fall by Angelico Press.
news.summary: About 10 years ago, I was quite sympathetic — empathetic, even — to the Black Lives Matter movement in its early days. That sympathy ran so deep that the first book I ever published, A Single Life, was a 2020 novel about a black Jewish protagonist, a character built to sit at the intersection of […]

news.title: Key races to be determined by minority of voters
news.text:
Hawaii’s next lieutenant governor, 65 state legislative seats, Office of Hawaiian Affairs trustees and local races in four Hawaii counties will likely be decided by a small share of registered voters — in a midterm election that’s unlikely to spark a surge in voter turnout.

Gov. Josh Green faces no high-profile challenge in his bid for a second term. And neither of Hawaii’s two U.S. senators are up for election this year.

So the marquee primary contests this year remain the Democratic nomination races for lieutenant governor and for the U.S. House seat to represent urban Honolulu.

U.S. Rep. Jill Tokuda has no high-profile Democratic challenger in the Aug. 8 Democratic Party primary to represent rural Oahu and the neighbor islands in Congress. Her likely opponent in the Nov. 3 general election appears to be Republican state Sen. Brenton Awa (R, Kaneohe--Mokuleia), who is making his first statewide run for political office.

The two most closely watched primary showdowns will be U.S. Rep. Ed Case’s reelection bid to represent urban Honolulu in Congress against state Sen. Jarrett Keohokalole (D, Kaneohe-Kailua), and state Rep. Della Au Belatti versus Kauai Mayor Derek Kawakami to become Hawaii’s next lieutenant governor. Bellati shook up Hawaii’s 2026 election in May when she dropped her long-shot bid to also challenge Case and instead jumped into the lieutenant governor’s race.

Kawakami appeared to have a relatively clear path to victory in his first statewide race until Belatti jumped in. She also has never launched a statewide campaign.

There are also competitive down-ballot races with all 51 state House seats and 14 of 25 Senate seats on the ballot and some County Council races that could spur political change.

Honolulu City Council Chair Tommy Waters faces a rematch against former Council member Trevor Ozawa, who lost a close and bitter race against Waters in 2019 that went all the way to the Hawaii Supreme Court, which ruled in Waters’ favor.

A separate lawsuit Ozawa filed last month remains pending, with a hearing scheduled July 27 before First Circuit Judge James Ashford. The complaint argues that Waters’ eligibility under the City Charter does not allow him to seek a third consecutive term. Ozawa also has filed a motion for summary judgment to hasten his lawsuit against Waters.

Former Council Chair Ikaika Anderson also is running for an at-large seat as a trustee for the Office of Hawaiian Affairs.

And there are other familiar names.

Former Kauai Mayor Bernard Carvalho Jr. is running for his old seat.

As is former state Rep. John Mizuno, who resigned his Kalihi House seat after 17 years in 2024 to serve as Gov. Josh Green’s homeless coordinator.

Green then named Mizuno’s wife and office manager, May Mizuno, to serve out the remainder of his term. But May Mizuno lost her first election bid two years ago when she was defeated by Rep. Ikaika Hussey.

Now John Mizuno will challenge Hussey in the Democratic Party primary to again represent Kamehameha Heights--Kalihi Valley.

Former state Rep. Bob McDermott, who served separate long stints in the House, is challenging a fellow Republican, state Sen. Kurt Fevella, to represent Ewa Beach-Ocean Pointe--Iroquois Point.

McDermott ended his second House term to unsuccessfully run to represent the neighbor islands and rural Oahu in Congress. He returned to the House in 
2012 only to resign again to lose a 2022 U.S. Senate race against Democrat Sen. Brian Schatz. McDermott lost again in 2024 as he tried to unseat Democratic U.S. Sen. Mazie Hirono.

State Rep. Jackson Sayama (D, Wilhelmina Rise-Maunalani Heights-St. Louis Heights) gave up his House seat this year to challenge veteran Sen. Les Ihara to represent Palolo-Kaimuki--Moiliili in the state Senate.

Two of Hawaii’s most colorful but so-far unsuccessful political candidates — former UFC mixed martial arts champion BJ Penn and comedian Bu La‘ia Hill — also have reentered the political arena.

In 1994, Hill ran for governor in the Democratic primary against eventual winner Ben Cayetano, and again in 2002 in the general election as a Natural Law Party nominee against Republican winner Linda Lingle.

Hill is now running his third race for governor as a nonpartisan candidate.

In 2004, Hill — whose real name is Shawn Kaui Hill — was running for OHA when he was sentenced to probation and community service for resisting arrest in an altercation at a Hilo flooring store. The following year a judge agreed to allow a deferral, and the convictions were removed from his record after he completed his probation.

Penn is running for Hawaii County’s District 1 Council seat. In 2022, Penn ran unsuccessfully to become the Republican nominee for governor.

Penn’s criminal record includes assaults, drunken driving and a no-contest plea in 2005 to assaulting a police officer. He also had multiple arrests in Hilo last year related to allegations of domestic abuse against his mother, refusal to comply with a lawful stay-away order and violating a temporary restraining order. Those cases are set for an 8:30 a.m. hearing on Aug. 28 in Hilo before Circuit Court Judge Peter Kubota.

There is little expectation that voters will turn out in droves for the party primaries following the 2024 presidential election year, when only 32.3% of all of Hawaii’s registered voters bothered to vote in the primary, a record low since widespread mail-in voting began in 2020.

The 2022 primary attracted only 39.8% of registered voters in a race that ultimately gave Hawaii voters a choice in the following November general election race between then-Lt. Gov. Josh Green and former Lt. Gov. Duke Aiona. The Democratic primary featured a bitter three-way fight between Green, former first lady Vicky Cayetano and former U.S. Rep. Kai Kahele, who now chairs the Office of Hawaiian Affairs.

The turnout in the last two primary elections suggests that residents across the islands remain motivated to register to vote, but most do not bother to actually cast their ballots.

Voter enthusiasm across the state has jumped when the stakes are high.

The anemic August 2024 turnout of 32.3% in the primary race soared to 60.7% in the subsequent November 2024 general election that saw then-former President Donald Trump and Vice President Kamala Harris battling to become president.

Before, in the November 2008 general election, a whopping 66% of registered Hawaii voters turned out when Oahu-born U.S. Sen. Barack Obama beat Republican Sen. John McCain to become president.
news.summary: Hawaii’s next lieutenant governor, 65 state legislative seats, Office of Hawaiian Affairs trustees and local races in four Hawaii counties will likely be decided by a small share of registered voters — in a midterm election that’s unlikely to spark a surge in voter turnout.
news.author: Dan Nakaso

news.title: ‘White men get to fail’: Graham Platner collapse illuminates toxic double standard in US politics
news.text:
Since Kamala Harris’s defeat to Donald Trump in 2024, Democrats have chased candidates who exude an ever-elusive “authenticity”. For many on the left, the answer was Graham Platner, a military veteran turned oyster farmer with a gravelly voice and deep hostility toward the political establishment.

Even as controversy after controversy emerged – racist, sexist, homophobic online posts; a tattoo widely recognized as a Nazi symbol, which he later covered up; sexually explicit messages sent to other women while he was married; and allegations from former partners of toxic and threatening behavior, which he denied – Platner’s momentum didn’t slow. It was only after a woman accused Platner of rape – which he denies – that his political support collapsed.

In the wake of his withdrawal, Democrats are asking themselves many questions, chief among them, who will replace him as their party’s nominee in the marquee Senate race? But the saga has also revived a deeper debate over whose flaws are forgivable – and whose are disqualifying.

“White men get to fail,” said Amanda Litman, co-founder of Run for Something, which recruits young, progressive candidates to run for office. “Women do not get to make mistakes. People of color do not get to make mistakes.”

Litman said the argument was not that serious allegations should be treated differently because of a candidate’s identity, but that Platner had been granted an unusual degree of latitude to recover from earlier controversies that a female candidate or a candidate of color would probably not have been afforded.

Litman said Platner was the “political equivalent of the Fyre fest guy or Adam Neuman” – high-profile men who continued to receive support for new ventures after spectacular failure. “It’s like there is inherent trust and faith in capabilities that they don’t have to prove,” she added.

Run for Something vets political candidates before offering their endorsement. As part of its endorsement application, Litman’s organization asks candidates to disclose what a background check might turn up, and what the opposition will find, Litman said.

After going through the vetting process, women and people of color are more likely to “self-censor” or decide they would rather not run at all, Litman said, adding: “The thing that I keep coming back to is, who has permission to be authentic?”

The result, according to candidates, strategists and scholars, is a political environment that often penalizes women and people of color more harshly than their white, male counterparts.

“Folks who have been identified as hardscrabble, populist, left-of-center white guys get far more leeway than folks like me, Black progressives,” Chris Rabb, who won a closely watched Democratic primary in a deep-blue Pennsylvania House district, said last month on the Breaking Points podcast. “And that’s a double standard that is largely based on race.”

Despite a record of contemptible behavior, Platner continued to raise large sums of money from grassroots donors, pushed a sitting governor out of the primary and cruised to the Democratic nomination.

“If I had some problematic tattoo that related to white folk, I don’t think I would be viable even in a majority-Black district,” Rabb added, “because I think the money would not be there to run the campaign I needed.”

When Platner’s campaign reached its ignominious end earlier this month, several Democratic women asked how a candidate with a record of contemptible behavior had gotten so far.

“I’m just a girl, running for the US Senate, with a medical degree, a record of service, and a non-problematic past,” Annie Andrews, the Democratic Senate nominee in South Carolina, wrote on X.

Andrews is a long shot in the Republican stronghold, thrown into its own turmoil after the death of Senator Lindsey Graham. But her tongue-in-cheek post captured a view widely shared among other women candidates: why him?

“Time after time, we have to watch strong women candidates get dismissed and treated like a backup plan, while some disastrous men take up all the oxygen – and ultimately make it harder to win the seats we need to take back our country,” Michelle White, the executive director of Emily’s List, said in a video, urging support for “qualified, inspiring Democratic women candidates”.

In Texas, the Democratic nominee for a state house seat, Sara McGee, divulged what she called her “deepest, darkest secrets”, among them a drunk-driving offense when she was a teenager, a divorce from a violent partner and “old debts that ended up in court when I was a single mom and couldn’t pay”.

“I was legitimately worried about these things tanking my chances of representing my community,” she wrote on X, expressing disbelief that some Democrats were willing to “accept the disqualifying characteristics of our own candidates because MAGA ‘did it worse’”.

When running for office, women, and particularly women of color, often start from a deficit, needing to prove not only that they are qualified but that they fit voters’ expectations of what leadership looks like, said Kelly Dittmar, a scholar at the Center for American Women in Politics at the Eagleton Institute of Politics at Rutgers University. “Who can best run authentically on all of their many identities are those whose identities are privileged across the board,” she said.

Dittmar pointed to research on how women decide whether to run in the first place. A 2008 survey of state legislators found women described their decision as “relationally embedded” – weighing how a campaign would affect their families, communities and careers – while men were more likely to run simply because they wanted the job.

Research has found that when women do run for office, they are more likely to face scrutiny over their personal lives. Women are held to a higher moral and ethical standard – a phenomenon the Barbara Lee Family Foundation called the “character pedestal”, in which voters expect women to be more honest and trustworthy than men. When women are accused of wrongdoing, “the fall from the pedestal is longer and harder”, Dittmar said, referencing the work of the Democratic pollster Celinda Lake.

As more women run for office, some see signs that political gender norms are changing, particularly since the 2016 presidential election, when the controversy over Hillary Clinton’s use of a private email server became a defining issue of her campaign – immortalized by the “but her emails” political meme – even as Trump faced a long record of controversies, including multiple accusations of sexual misconduct.

On the left, voters are bucking convention and embracing a new crop of unabashedly progressive – and decidedly aggressive – candidates. Darializa Avila Chevalier, a democratic socialist and the daughter of immigrants from the Dominican Republic, succeeded in toppling a senior Democratic incumbent in a New York congressional primary despite scrutiny over social media posts she had deleted, but had been archived. In the posts, she frequently and profanely disparaged the Democratic party and its leaders, including Kamala Harris, questioned the origins of Covid-19 and criticized interracial relationships.

As a first-time political candidate, Kat Abughazaleh spent months worrying that someone would unearth her “middle school livejournal fanfic account”.

“I’m still amazed it didn’t happen,” Abughazaleh, who lost the Democratic primary for an Illinois house seat earlier this year, wrote on Bluesky.

In an interview, Abughazaleh recalled her decision to reveal that she has narcolepsy, a disorder that disrupts sleep cycles and can lead to extreme daytime sleepiness. She faced several days of articles and comments online, including people who called it a “TikTok illness”.

“This is a serious, debilitating illness that I try to be transparent about because we have shitty disability rights in this country,” she said. “But I don’t have a fucking Nazi tattoo. I can promise you, I’ve never raped anyone.”

As Democrats search for candidates they hope can lead the party back to power in November, Abughazaleh hopes the combustible end of Platner’s campaign will prompt the party to widen its hunt for “authentic” candidates.

“God damn,” she said. “Can we please have people besides just white guys running for office?”
news.summary:
Expectations that prioritize privileged identities keep women and people of color from even running for office

Since Kamala Harris’s defeat to Donald Trump in 2024, Democrats have chased candidates who exude an ever-elusive “authenticity”. For many on the left, the answer was Graham Platner, a military veteran turned oyster farmer with a gravelly voice and deep hostility toward the political establishment.

Even as controversy after controversy emerged – racist, sexist, homophobic online posts; a tattoo widely recognized as a Nazi symbol, which he later covered up; sexually explicit messages sent to other women while he was married; and allegations from former partners of toxic and threatening behavior, which he denied – Platner’s momentum didn’t slow. It was only after a woman accused Platner of rape – which he denies – that his political support collapsed.

 Continue reading...
